title: The Light Rule
html:
The question Jiang Wenxu needed answered was not whether Chen Shu had passed the west-end figure without being triggered, but how exactly he had done it — not a general account but the specific geometry.

"When you went out this morning," he said. "Which wall were you on."

Chen Shu looked at the door gap. "The right one," he said, and then after a moment: "The wall furthest from it."

"How far from the figure when you were closest to it."

Chen Shu thought about it. The pause had the quality of someone reconstructing a memory they'd spent hours trying not to examine. "I don't know exactly. I was moving fast and keeping to the wall. I didn't look at it directly."

"Did you pass under the burned section of ceiling."

Chen Shu turned his head and looked at him with an expression that was half confusion and half the beginning of understanding something he hadn't understood until just now. "The light above it is out," he said.

"Yes."

Chen Shu looked at the door gap, at the sliver of corridor visible through it: the right wall, the even institutional lighting, and at the far end the single dark section of ceiling above the motionless white figure. "I stayed under the working lights," he said slowly. "I didn't think about it, I just — the lit side of the corridor was the far wall from the figure, so I used the far wall." He paused. "I stayed in the light."

Jiang Wenxu checked the wall clock through the door gap: 8:58. He'd been in this building for approximately 1 hour and 44 minutes, and from inside 7-C the west corridor ceiling had been visible through the window boards only at a narrow angle that showed the exterior, not the interior fixtures. He hadn't seen the west corridor lights until he stepped into the west corridor 11 minutes ago. The burned-out fixture above the figure was a detail he'd registered without fully integrating because he'd been focused on the figure itself.

Item 5: do not turn off the common area lights without staff authorization.

Not: do not damage the lights, or interfere with the building's electrical system. The specific verb was turn off, an action a person performs deliberately on a working light, which meant the rule existed because working lights being on had a specific protective function worth encoding as a prohibition. A light already burned out was not covered by item 5. The west-end figure was standing in a zone where that function no longer applied.

'The light rule,' he thought. Not a rule about behavior in the dark. A rule about maintaining the conditions that made the lit parts of the corridor safe.

He looked at the corridor through the door gap one more time, checking the right wall from 7-A to the stairwell door: 3 working fixtures, all on, even coverage from the doorframe all the way to within about 1 meter of the stairwell entry. The dark section above the figure started at approximately 4 meters from the stairwell door and extended back 3 or 4 meters. If you walked the right wall and stayed under the working lights, you would be in light for the full length of the corridor except possibly the last meter before the stairwell.

[He's been staring at the door gap for 4 minutes]

[The kid from overnight said something and now the new player is doing his spreadsheet face again]

[Stream is at 340 viewers. Someone clipped the 8-B door opening and it's getting shared]

[Everyone in the clip comments is asking the same question which is: who IS this guy]

[First instance player, for anyone who missed it. First instance.]

Song Yu was watching him.

Not in the incidental way of people who occupy the same small room — he'd been watching for several minutes with the quality of attention that isn't casual observation but assessment, working through something with Jiang Wenxu as the subject. When Jiang Wenxu turned from the door gap, Song Yu held his position against the wall with the pipe at his side and said: "You've been right about everything since I met you."

Jiang Wenxu waited.

"The knocking. The key. The direction." Song Yu's voice was at corridor-quiet, which made the flatness of it more apparent, not less. "I've cleared 2 instances before this one. I have notes on 40 different instance types. I walked into this building and spent 2 hours going the wrong way." He paused. "You walked in an hour ago and you've been right about everything."

The internal assessment Jiang Wenxu ran in approximately 2 seconds was this: Song Yu was not angry, or not only angry, and the question underneath the statement wasn't 'what do you know that I don't' so much as 'what am I missing that means a first-instance player is reading this better than me.' It was a more honest version of the question and it deserved a more honest answer.

"I didn't have prior instances to compare this to," he said. "You pattern-matched to instance types you'd already cleared. I read the specific sign that was in front of me."

Song Yu stared at him for a moment. "That's it," he said.

"Yes."

It was impossible to determine from Song Yu's expression whether he believed this or had filed it under a different hypothesis and was choosing not to share the hypothesis. Jiang Wenxu had limited data on Song Yu's face as a readable object. He set this aside and turned back to the door gap.

[Song Yu finally asked]

[And the answer is "I read the sign more carefully" which is either completely true or the most suspicious thing I've ever heard]

[It's completely true. He literally just re-read the sign until he got more out of it. I watched him do it for like 40 minutes in Chapter 1]

[Song Yu doesn't look like he fully believes it]

[Can you blame him lol]

He laid out the light theory in 4 sentences: item 5 protects working lights because lit zones are safe zones; the west-end figure stands under the one burned-out fixture; the safe zone ends where the light ends; Chen Shu passed it by staying under working lights on the far wall without knowing that was what he was doing. Song Yu listened without interrupting. When Jiang Wenxu finished, Song Yu was quiet for 3 seconds.

"The stairwell," Song Yu said.

"Dark, probably. I don't know yet."

Song Yu's expression shifted slightly and in a different direction than Jiang Wenxu expected: not skepticism but recognition. "Stairwells in facilities like this have emergency lighting panels," he said. "Pull-cord or switch, mounted near the entry. If the stairwell light is out it's because whoever set this instance up made sure it was, but there may still be a panel." He paused. "That's not from reading a sign. That's from a previous instance that used the same building type."

There was a specific quality to the way Song Yu said this — offering information, marking it clearly as his, watching for how it was received. Not generosity exactly. An exchange, calibrated.

"Where would the panel be," Jiang Wenxu said.

"Interior left wall, within 2 meters of the door. Building code."

He filed this.

At 9:07 AM he stepped out of 7-A and turned right, which put him against the far wall of the west corridor immediately. The right wall. The lit side. The 3 working fixtures ran above him in an even line and the floor under each was the same pale linoleum as the rest of the corridor, without the slight gray shift he'd noticed under the east-wing lights. He walked at a normal pace.

The west-end figure was 25 meters ahead. Its back was to him. It had not moved in the approximately 2 hours he'd been monitoring it at intervals.

He watched it as he walked. The internal version of what he was doing was considerably less composed than the external version: he was very aware of the distance between himself and the dark section of ceiling, and the part of him that had been cataloging every involuntary physiological response since he woke up in this building had already noted that his pulse was at a different rate than it had been in 7-A. He filed this and continued walking.

At 10 meters from the figure the corridor narrowed slightly at a junction he hadn't seen from the door gap, and the right wall bent 20 cm inward for a section of about 3 meters before returning to its original line. The bend put him momentarily closer to the corridor center. He adjusted his angle and stayed as far right as the wall allowed.

He passed the figure at a distance of approximately 5 meters. The dark section of ceiling was to his left, over the figure, and the working fixture above his head was on. The figure did not move. The smell from this proximity was stronger than it had been through the door gap: the cleaning chemical and the thing beneath it, both more present at 5 meters than at 30. He kept his pace and did not look at the figure directly.

The stairwell door was 8 meters further. He reached it, found the push bar, and pressed through.

[He's walking. Just walking. Past it.]

[It's not moving]

[Stay in the light. HE'S STAYING IN THE LIGHT]

[500 viewers. Stream jumped while he was walking]

[The theory worked. The THEORY WORKED]

The stairwell was dark.

Not the east-wing dark with its gray-cast light and reduced visibility, and not the deep black of the grounds outside at night. This was a different quality of dark — flat and dense, the kind that arrived at the top of the stairs and stopped there, not dispersing into the corridor light from the door he'd just pushed through. The landing was a 2-meter square of concrete where the door light reached, and below the first step it was complete.

He stood at the top and looked down. One flight. A landing at the bottom where the lobby was. He couldn't see the landing. The sound he'd expected from the ventilation or the building's general low frequency wasn't what reached him from below: it was a specific sound, periodic, occurring at uneven intervals that could have been 8 seconds apart or 12, the kind of interval that a thing produces when it is not tracking time but simply present. Not footsteps. Not the doubled cadence of the staff figures. A different category.

He looked at the left wall inside the door. The emergency lighting panel was there, a small grey box at shoulder height with a pull-cord and a yellow plastic handle. He pulled the cord and the stairwell stayed dark. The cord moved freely when pulled, so the mechanism wasn't jammed; the light simply didn't come on, which meant either the power to this panel had been cut or the stairwell fixture was burned out the same way the west corridor fixture above the figure was burned out.

He did not go down the stairs.

He came back through the stairwell door, crossed the corridor on the right wall with the figure still motionless 5 meters to his left, returned to 7-A, and shut the door with his hand flat against the frame. 9:13 AM.

Song Yu read his face, which was to say he read nothing useful from it, and waited.

"Stairwell is dark," Jiang Wenxu said. "The emergency panel is there but it doesn't work. Something is in the stairwell. I don't know what."

"Something in it," Song Yu said.

"Audible from the landing. Periodic. Not a staff figure cadence."

Chen Shu had pressed himself further against the back wall of 7-A during the 6 minutes Jiang Wenxu had been gone, which was a visible record of what those 6 minutes had felt like from inside the room. He said, voice still roughened: "What does it sound like."

Jiang Wenxu thought about how to describe it accurately. "Something that's in a space and not moving through it," he said.

The room was quiet for a moment.

He needed a light source for the stairwell. The corridor lights were working fixtures protected by item 5 and were not removable without violating an explicit prohibition. The stairwell emergency panel was nonfunctional. The building had no other light sources he'd identified, with one exception: the Collection Station in room 8-B had a laminated card on a lanyard hanging from a hook, and on the desk beside the key he'd taken there had been a logbook. He hadn't opened it.

A logbook in a facility like this was a record of rounds, maintenance notes, incident reports — information about the building's operational state written by whoever had run this place. If the stairwell light had been deliberately disabled as Song Yu suspected, and if this building had a logbook that covered maintenance records, there might be a notation about it. There might also be a notation about the emergency panel, or about what the stairwell contained, or about the west-end figure's behavior parameters. There might be nothing useful. He couldn't determine which from 7-A.

"The logbook in the Collection Station," he said. "We didn't open it."

Song Yu looked at him. "We have to go back through the east wing."

"Yes."

A pause, shorter than Jiang Wenxu expected. "I know the 8-B knock now," Song Yu said. "I can do it."

[He walked past the thing, found the stairwell, and came back in 6 minutes]

[The stairwell is dark and there's something IN it]

[And his plan is to go back to the other wing for a BOOK]

[Okay but he also correctly identified the light rule, correctly passed the figure, correctly found the stairwell in one attempt. At this point I'd trust him to go back for a book.]

[Song Yu just offered to run the east wing again. That's. Song Yu is DIFFERENT now.]

[Two hours ago Song Yu was the one with the plan and this guy was the new player following him. Now look at this]

Chen Shu said: "There's something else."

Both of them looked at him.

He'd straightened slightly from the wall, which was a small change that made a visible difference to how much of the room he occupied. "Last night. After 10 PM. There was a patient."

Jiang Wenxu waited.

"In the corridor. Not a staff figure — different sound, slower, and it stopped outside rooms. It knocked." Chen Shu looked at his hands. "It knocked the way you knocked on my door. Not the metronomic kind."

Item 3: do not attempt to communicate with unresponsive patients. Not staff figures. Patients. The sign distinguished between two categories of entity in this building, and all the entities Jiang Wenxu had encountered so far were the staff category. A patient who knocked on doors at night, after 10 PM, when the building's behavior changed — that was a different category, with different rules, and item 3's specific qualifier of unresponsive suddenly acquired a function it hadn't had before.

"Did you answer it," he said.

"No." Chen Shu's voice was flat. "I didn't know if I was supposed to."

'Responsive,' Jiang Wenxu thought. Item 3 said don't communicate with unresponsive patients. It didn't say don't communicate with responsive ones. A patient knocking on doors and waiting for an answer was, by the precise language of the sign, responsive. Which meant communicating with it was not prohibited. Which meant it might be something that could be communicated with.

He checked the wall clock through the gap: 9:16 AM. He looked at Song Yu.

"We go back for the logbook," he said. "And tonight we answer the door."

Song Yu looked at him for a moment and said nothing. Chen Shu looked at the door gap. The survival probability notification occupied its usual position in the right edge of Jiang Wenxu's vision, still calculating, still producing nothing, a background process that had been running since the instance began and seemed to have every intention of continuing.
